entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud compass in Q1 2026 - New Use Cases, Enhanced Workflow
entry.content:
2026 is going to be an exciting year for compass! For fans of Monte Carlo Simulation and scenario modelling in SAP Analytics Cloud, we shall kick the year off with three highly requested enhancements for Q1:

  1. Supporting AVG excl. NULL and AVG incl. unbooked for drivers
  2. Enabling simulation from dashboards with complex filters
  3. Enabling changing of target settings after simulation creation

Let me walk you through them:

 

AVG excl. NULL and AVG incl. unbooked for drivers

Previously, users were only able to model the uncertainty of key drivers with SUM aggregation or SUM exception aggregation. Even though this applies for most key drivers, users wishing to simulate with averages were left out of the game. The new support of AVG excl. NULL and AVG incl. unbooked for drivers will open the door now to simulations with average prices, average discount, average hours or any KPI which should not be summed up to reflect accurate performance assessment. This means that simulation for use cases such sales promotions or energy expenses etc. are now possible.  
(If you are unsure about the differences between the different AVG variants, check out this great blog from Alecsandra Dimofte.)


Before the enhancement, key drivers modelled with AVG excl. NULL or incl. unbooked will be blocked from simulation. 


Now users will be able to model uncertainties for key drivers modelled with AVG excl. NULL or incl. unbooked.

 

Enabling simulation from dashboards with complex filters

In the past, complex filters applied on the story, page or table level would block the creation of compass simulations directly from the story table. These complex filters include:

  • Attribute
  • Range
  • Exclude
  • Advanced filter

With the latest enhancement, the restriction is lifted and users could directly create a compass simulation right off the dashboard even with this setup. Dashboard creators, especially those dependent on time range filters or exclude filter for ease of selection, will benefit from the flexibility this feature brings to their story design to provide end users with a direct trigger for compass simulation. Take note that the complex filters applied will be displayed as read-only in compass though. Users are free to simulate right away in compass or to substitute the complex filters with something simpler before proceeding, if preferred.

Range filters and other complex filters will no longer block the direct trigger of a compass simulation from a story table. 

 

Enabling changing of target settings after simulation creation

Before Q1 2026, it was not possible to make adjustments to a compass simulation target setting once a scenario has been created. Any changes to the target setting after simulation creation would require manual removal of existing scenarios, as it would be a change in the simulation calculation. 
With the latest enhancement, the workflow has become more flexible. Users can change the target settings at any point in time. After applying the changes, the prior simulation results of private scenarios will be discarded (as the prior calculation will differ from that of the current target settings), but the driver value configurations will be retained. Users seeking to simulate impact on another similar target KPI will benefit from the ability to continue right away without having to set up identical assumptions for common drivers again.
Public scenarios, on the other hand, will be marked as invalid, but both results and driver value configurations will be retained, in case there is a need to archive past simulations despite the difference in target settings. 

 

Simulation target settings can be changed now even after scenario creation. Driver value configurations will be retained in the private scenarios.

 

With these three new enhancements in Q1 2026, compass users will experience more flexibility in handling, with better re-use of assumption modelling efforts, and more variety in the spectrum of possible drivers.

As usual, we are always on the look out for areas where compass could be sharpened even further. Have an opinion here? Do tell us what you think in the influence portal! In fact, our first feature in Q1 2026 was inspired by the a topic raised by @David_Nicolay_ . Thank you and do keep them coming!

entry.title: What’s New on My Metrics 2026 Q1 QRC: Schedule a Metrics Report
entry.content:
In my last blog post, I announced the release of scheduled Metrics Reports for 2026 Q1 QRC, and now it’s time to take a closer look at how they help you stay on top of your data.

Keeping an eye on performance metrics is essential but manually checking them can quickly become time-consuming. With scheduled Metrics Reports in My Metrics, you can stay informed without the extra effort. By subscribing to a metrics report, you’ll automatically receive updates on changes in your selected metrics, delivered on a daily, weekly, or monthly schedule. Simply choose the recurrence pattern that works best for you and let the insights come to you right when you need them.

Below is a practical guide to what the feature does, how to set it up, common options, and tips for best results.

Why Subscribe to a Metrics Report?

Subscribing to a metrics report lets you receive regular, automated updates on metric changes without having to manually check stories. It ensures that teams and stakeholders get consistent snapshots of what has changed since the previous report, while giving you control over the delivery cadence and recipients, so insights arrive exactly when they’re most useful.

What You Need Before You Start 

  • Access to My Metrics and permission to create subscriptions (check your role settings).
  • If you want recurrence based on working days, you must have a working day calendar defined in the underlying model. For details, see Use a Working Day Calendar for Your Series.
  • Only metrics that come from tunnel connection type models are supported in the report (this connection allows controlled exposure of data outside the corporate network). For more information, see Live Data Connections to SAP S/4HANA.
  • This feature is only available in SAP Analytics Cloud tenants running in the Cloud Foundry environment (not available in the Neo environment).

Quick Step-by-Step Setup

  1. Open the metrics list you want to subscribe to in My Metrics.
  2. In list view, choose More (the ••• / menu) and pick Subscribe to Metrics Report.
  3. In the creation dialog, enter a Subscription Name (mandatory) and a Description (recommended). Note: the name can contain only letters and numbers no special characters. 
  4. Click Next to choose the schedule.
  5. Define the recurrence pattern: Day, Week, or Month. If you use Working Day(s), pick the model that contains your working day calendar:
    • Day
      • Choose Day(s) or Working Day(s) and enter the frequency number (e.g., every 2 days).
      • If you choose Working Day(s), select the model and calendar to define non-working days
    • Week
      • Set the number of weeks between reports.
      • Choose one or more weekdays to run the report, or select Working Day and pick an ordinal working day (first, second, third, fourth, or last) from the calendar.
      • When using Working Day, select the appropriate model and calendar if more than one is available. 
    • Month
      • Day option: specify a day number (1–31). If the month has fewer days than the number you entered, the report runs on the month’s last day.
      • Working Day option: enter a frequency number or choose an ordinal working day (first, second, third, fourth, or last) of the month; select the model/calendar if required.
      • You can also schedule by “nth weekday” (for example, the second Tuesday of each month).
      • Examples: entering -2 schedules the report for the second-to-last day of the month; entering 31 schedules it on the month’s last day. 
  6. Set the planned start date and time and when the recurrence should end (by end date or number of occurrences).
  7. Click Next and add at least one recipient in the Distribution List. You can select existing users or teams.
  8. Optionally uncheck “Send email notifications” if you prefer in-app notifications onlybelow an example of an email notification of a Metrics Report:                                                                                                 
  9. Save to create the subscription.

Other Important Details and Limitations

  • Each metrics report can include up to 15 metrics.
  • The report only includes metrics that have changed since their last recorded value, this focuses recipients on recent movement.
  • Sent reports are also accessible from in-app notifications.
  • If you set recurrence on working day calendars, no report is sent on non-working days defined by that calendar.

Manage subscriptions

Manage Subscriptions lets recipients control how they interact with a metrics report and lets owners copy, edit, or delete subscriptions. Recipients can copy a subscription or change their notification settings; only the subscription owner can edit or delete it. You can also preview subscription details from the Manage Subscriptions dialog. Note that you need the correct permissions to perform these actions.

Accessing and filtering subscriptions

  • Open the metrics list in My Metrics, then choose More → Manage Subscriptions.
  • Use the filter to show Active Subscriptions (default) or switch to All Subscriptions to see inactive ones as well. This helps you quickly find the subscription you want to manage.

Copying a subscription

  • Select a subscription in the Manage Subscriptions dialog and choose Copy Subscription.
  • When you copy a subscription you duplicate its details but can change schedule or recipients. You become the owner of the new copy and it is independent of the original.

Modifying your notification settings

Recipients can mute or resume notifications for a subscription without affecting others. Steps:

  • Open the subscription, choose Notification Settings, then pick:
    • Resume Notifications
    • Mute Until: select a date and time to pause notifications until then
    • Mute All Scheduled Reports
  • Save changes. Your choices apply only to your account and won’t change other recipients’ settings.

Editing a subscription (owner only)

Owners can edit name, description, schedule, and distribution list. Steps:

  • Open Manage Subscriptions, select the subscription, choose Edit Subscription, make changes, and save.
  • Note: changing a subscription may trigger a new report schedule; recipients will be affected by edits.

Deleting a subscription (owner only)

  • Owners can delete one or more subscriptions from the Manage Subscriptions dialog.
  • Deletion removes all recipients and cancels scheduled reports. Confirm deletion in the warning dialog to proceed.

Previewing subscription details

  • In Manage Subscriptions, select a subscription to view its details (description, schedule, end date, owner, distribution list).
  • Use Preview to verify settings before copying, editing, or deleting.

Best Practices

Use descriptive subscription names in the Description field to clearly communicate the purpose and intended audience, since the subscription name itself supports only alphanumeric characters. Before rolling it out broadly, test the subscription with a small group of recipients to validate the timing and content. If your reports depend on working days, make sure the model’s working day calendar is correctly configured and that the appropriate calendar is selected when multiple options are available. Finally, choose notification channels thoughtfully, use in-app notifications for teams working primarily in SAC, and enable email notifications for external stakeholders or wider visibility.

You can quickly find and act on the correct subscriptions by using filters, making it easier to manage them efficiently. If you need similar reports, you can copy an existing subscription without changing the original setup. Notifications can be muted temporarily when a report is sent too frequently and resumed later as needed. To prevent confusion, owners should clearly communicate any edits or deletions to recipients. If management options are not visible, it’s important to verify permissions, as available actions depend on assigned roles.

Troubleshooting Tips

  • If recipients don’t receive reports, confirm you’re on a Cloud Foundry tenant and that recipients’ email addresses or user accounts are correct.
  • If a metric you expect is missing, check that it’s based on a tunnel connection type model and that it changed since the last report.
  • If the schedule doesn’t behave as expected with working days, review the model’s working day calendar and the selected calendar if multiple calendars are maintained.
  • If you can’t edit or delete a subscription, confirm you are the owner and have the required role.
  • If notifications don’t change after updating settings, re-open Manage Subscriptions to confirm your saved preferences.
  • If recipients stop receiving reports after edits, check the distribution list and schedule settings.

Wrapping Up

Subscribing to a metrics report in My Metrics is a simple way to keep stakeholders informed about metric changes with flexible scheduling and notification options. With attention to the working day settings and model selection, you can align report delivery to business calendars and avoid unnecessary messages on non-working days.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud - Consolidated Error Messages in SAC Story Designer
entry.content:
An Efficiency Boost for SAC Story Designers: The New Consolidated Info Panel with the 2026.Q1 (2026.01) Release

Hello fellow SAC Story Designers,

Anyone who has already worked with the new "Model Repointing" feature knows how powerful it is. It offers enormous flexibility, but it also brings with it a challenge: after repointing a model, there is a significant risk that objects in the story will "break" - be it due to unmapped bindings, changed hierarchies, or other inconsistencies.

Previously, this meant one had to tediously navigate through every page and widget to re-establish confidence that the dashboard was in a proper, consumable state. This process was not only time-consuming but also prone to error.

For exactly this scenario, and similar ones, a decisive improvement has been introduced within the Story Design Time: the new, consolidated Info Panel. It is designed to become your central point of control for quality assurance.

The Old Way: Fragmented Information

Let us remember: Issues and recommendations were displayed in various places:

  • Critical errors (for example, a broken data binding) often appeared as individual toast messages or small icons on the widget itself.
  • Performance recommendations were somewhat hidden in a separate popup or in tooltips.

The result was a fragmented overview where one had to actively hunt for problems and could easily miss something.

The New Way: One Panel for Everything

The new Info Panel, which you can access via the icon in the top toolbar, breaks with this fragmentation. It consolidates all relevant messages in one location, giving you a clear and immediate overview of your story's health.

 

Caption: The new, consolidated Info Panel with its 'Issues', 'Performance Recommendations', and 'Scripting Errors' tabs.

Let us look at the decisive improvements in more detail:

The "Issues" Tab: The New Core Component

The most important new area is the "Issues" tab. Here, all asset-related problems are centrally collected and intelligently structured:

  • Clear Categorization: The messages can be filtered by severity (Error, Warning, Info) and are color-coded, so you see immediately what is critical.
  • Logical Structure: The list is grouped hierarchically by page and widget. You can see at a glance which chart on which page is affected.

Typical issues that you will find here are, for example:

  • Missing Input Control: A control element was deleted that is required by a chart.
  • Invalid Calculation: A calculation is no longer valid because it depends on another, missing calculation.

Integrated Performance and Scripting Insights

Next to the "Issues" tab, you find the familiar sections, which are now, however, seamlessly integrated:

  • Performance Recommendations: The proactive tips for optimizing widget performance are now also part of this central panel. Consequently, you have performance and functional issues in one view. Please check also our separate blogpost on this topic SAP Business Data Cloud - Performance Recommendations for Story Designers
  • Scripting Errors: For all who work in "Advanced Mode," scripting errors are also consolidated here, which simplifies the debugging of complex interactions considerably.

The Concrete Value for Your Daily Workflow

  • Security after Model Repointing: Instead of a manual search, you simply open the Info Panel and receive a complete list of all problems that resulted from the repointing.
  • Significant Time Savings in Debugging: The direct action links ("Open Builder Panel") reduce the click paths and search effort to a minimum.
  • Increased Story Quality: You will no longer overlook warnings or "smaller" problems and can therefore ensure a consistently high quality of your dashboards.
  • A More Efficient Workflow: All information relevant to the story's health is bundled in one place. No more jumping between different menus and views is necessary.

Conclusion

The new, consolidated Info Panel is more than just a cosmetic update. It is a fundamentally improved tool that directly addresses the pain points of us, the story designers. It proves its value especially in scenarios like Model Repointing, by helping us to quickly and reliably maintain control over complex stories.

It is a small but highly effective step towards a cleaner and more efficient design process.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud - Management of Classic Stories Before Deprecation
entry.content:
Targeted Management of Classic Stories Before Deprecation with the 2026.Q1 (2026.02) Release

Hello everyone,

As many of you know, a significant technical transition is on the horizon for SAP Analytics Cloud: with the Q2 2026 release, "Classic Stories" will be officially deprecated. From this point on, it will no longer be possible to open or edit stories based on the old stack. The path forward is the conversion of this content to the "Optimized Story" format.

To support you in this process, we have previously provided tools that identify which Classic Stories are still actively in use. This approach of targeted conversion has helped many of you to set priorities (you can read more about this in our blog post on Efficient Migration of SAC Classic Stories: Focusing on the Essentials).

However, based on your feedback, we are now taking this a significant step further. Instead of just focusing on stories that are in use, we're now giving you the tools to get a complete overview and proactively clean up your system.

What's New? Three Key Enhancements for Your System Management

To make the transition as smooth as possible, we have specifically enhanced three areas of SAP Analytics Cloud:

1. A Complete, Exportable List of All Classic Stories

In the System > Overview > Content area, you will now find a new, comprehensive table. This table lists all Classic Stories present in your system—regardless of whether they have been used recently or not. The key advantage: this list is fully exportable. You can easily load the data into Excel or another tool to sort, filter, and align with business departments.

2. Targeted Deletion of Classic Stories in Housekeeping

A clean system is a performant system. We have enhanced the System > Overview > Housekeeping area to allow you to specifically filter for Classic Stories and delete them directly, even in bulk. This builds upon the existing housekeeping functionalities and provides you with a powerful tool for system hygiene. (For a general introduction to this topic, you can refer to our Efficient Data Maintenance in SAP Analytics Cloud System Overview - Housekeeping.)

 

 

3. Enhanced Metadata for Custom Analyses

For those who want to perform deeper analyses or build their own monitoring dashboards, we have enhanced the underlying data model, known as the 'Resource' model. This model is part of the standard monitoring content that is automatically updated with every system update. You can find this content under the path Files > System > Common > SAC Content.

Our recommendation is to use the standard dashboards provided there to track the progress of your migration efforts.

However, if you wish to build your own analyses or tables to specifically list Classic Stories, you can do so directly on the 'Resource' data model. To get a precise list, apply the following filters to the data source:

  • Resource Version: CLASSIC
  • Object Type: STORY
  • Resource Subtype: (No Value)

 

These filters ensure that you are exclusively looking at Classic Stories and not other object types or subtypes, such as bookmarks, in your analysis.

A Recommended Workflow

  1. Inventory: Export the complete list of Classic Stories from the System > Overview > Content area.
  2. Analysis & Decision: Go through the list and classify each story: Convert or Delete?
  3. Execution:
    1. Convert the necessary stories to the Optimized Story experience.
    2. Use the new feature in Housekeeping to remove the obsolete stories.
  4. Monitoring: Keep track of your progress, either with the standard content found under Files > System > Common > SAC Content or with your own custom analysis.

Summary

The Q2 2026 deadline is set. With a complete overview, targeted deletion capabilities, and enhanced metadata, you now have full control over the process. We recommend starting to familiarize yourself with these new tools early to ensure a smooth transition and to start the future with a clean system.

entry.title: Sneak Peek in to SAP Analytics Cloud release for Q1 2026
entry.content:
As we move toward the end of January we take a sneak peek in to our Top 5 features in SAP Analytics Cloud for QRC1 2026. A full list of what is coming can be found on our Roadmap Explorer.  Let's dive in!

New Chart Types

To kick off the year we are expanding the native visualization capabilities available with the introduction of new Pareto and area chart types designed to simplify advanced visualization and analysis. The Pareto chart automatically performs 80/20 analysis using built‑in running‑total logic and allows the addition of comparison measures without affecting the core calculation. The new area chart enhances trend exploration through more intuitive visual breakdowns.

Together, these new chart types reduce reliance on custom widgets, streamline dashboard creation, and mark the beginning of a broader roadmap to deliver more out‑of‑the‑box visualizations that elevate analytical flexibility and user experience.

 

Story Versioning

Staying with Story enhancements this quarter we introduce a new story versioning framework which enables designers to create, manage, and restore up to ten major versions of a story, providing greater control over iterative development. Story Designers can access version history, open previous versions in a read-only format, restore them, or save them as new artifacts, supporting safe experimentation without risking published content.

This capability ensures transparency in change tracking and simplifies collaboration across teams.

As the foundational phase of a larger multi artifact versioning roadmap, it establishes the technical basis for extending version control to additional SAP Analytics Cloud asset types in future releases.

 

 

Comment Management

We know how important collaboration is for our customers, so this quarter we have introduced a new comment management dashboard that enables a centralized and streamlined way to to manage comments across SAP Analytics Cloud models and versions. This gives Administrators visibility into comment statistics, including threads, data‑point comments, and dimension comment, enabling more effective governance and auditability.

Bulk actions, such as deleting comments at a version or model level and copying comments across versions within Model, simplify administrative workflows and help maintain clean, compliant environments.

 

 

My Metrics for KPIs monitoring: Alerting 

Continuing to enhance our "My Metrics" framework introduced introduced at the end of 2025 with lots of new features for an even more personalized experience. These include:

  • Subscribe or unsubscribe to metrics reports for continuous change tracking
  • Set report frequency by day, week, or month
  • Share reports with other users or teams 
  • Receive reports by e-mail or through the notification center 

 

 

Live data connectivity to Snowflake

In this release we are excited to announce that SAP Analytics Cloud has extended its external live data access for planning capable models to Snowflake.

This connection reduces data loading overhead, simplifies model configuration, and lowers total cost of ownership by allowing customers to work directly with Snowflake data as a live external version. Once the data access agent is enabled, users can create planning capable models based on Snowflake sources, benefiting from the same performance considerations and modeling flexibility available for existing live connections such as Google BigQuery.

 

There are plenty more exciting deliverables coming your way this quarter and we will be taking a deeper dive into these items in our What’s New video and blog series so stay tuned. For a full list of all features coming in QRC1 2026 for SAP Analytics Cloud check out the Roadmap Explorer  and the What's New Viewer

Want to experience new and existing features for yourself? Take the leap and start your journey towards making data-driven decisions with confidence by signing up for a free trial, today.

entry.title: [BDC-SAC] Akamai CDN , Metadata Understanding Data Download When Access SAC
entry.content:
Understanding Data Download Behavior When Accessing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)

When accessing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) through a web browser, several external URLs are involved.
At a high level, these can be categorized into static content delivered via CDN and dynamic, tenant-specific metadata.

Static Content: Assets and SAPUI5 via Akamai CDN
By default, SAC loads static resources such as:

  • Assets
  • SAPUI5 libraries
    These resources are delivered through the Akamai CDN.
    Because they are static and globally distributed, they are highly optimized for performance and reuse.


Dynamic Content: Tenant-Specific Metadata
In addition to static content, SAC also retrieves metadata associated with the customer tenant, such as:

  • User- and role-related metadata
  • Model and story metadata
  • Session-related configuration data

These requests are typically served directly from SAP data centers rather than the CDN.

A Common Korean Customer Question: “How Much Data Is Downloaded Every Time?”

A frequent question arises around the relative data volume downloaded from Akamai CDN (static content) vs. Data centers (dynamic metadata)

This question often includes the phrase “data downloaded every time”, which can be interpreted in multiple ways and is sometimes misunderstood.
Why “Downloaded Every Time” Is Misleading
In practice, data is not downloaded on every access in the literal sense:

  • Browser caching plays a critical role
  • Cached data may reside in memory cache or disk cache
  • Even when cached, loading data from cache still incurs some processing time, but it does not involve network download

As a result, Static content served via Akamai CDN is rarely re-downloaded. Dynamic metadata is also not fully re-downloaded on every request, but fetched incrementally and conditionally based on session state and cache validation

What HAR File Analysis Typically Shows
When analyzing HAR files, a consistent pattern emerges:

  • In terms of request volume and payload size, static content delivered via Akamai CDN accounts for the majority of data transferred
  • Dynamic metadata requests are generally smaller in size and more selective

    This means that, from a network and performance perspective, Akamai-delivered static resources dominate the overall traffic profile, especially during initial access or first-time load scenarios.

Architectural Implication: Regional Tenants and Legal Entities

This observation has an important architectural implication. For customers operating multiple legal entities across regions, there is often concern that:
“Separating tenants by region may negatively impact network traffic or performance.”

However, given that:

  • The majority of transferred data is static and CDN-delivered
  • Static content is shared and globally optimized
  • Tenant-specific metadata represents a smaller portion of total traffic

There is no inherent disadvantage—from a data transfer or performance standpoint—in operating multiple regional tenants, when required for legal, regulatory, or organizational reasons.


Understanding how SAC content is delivered—and how browser caching actually works—helps clarify common misconceptions around “repeated downloads.”

In reality:

  • Most data is not repeatedly downloaded
  • Akamai CDN plays a dominant role in overall data transfer
  • Tenant separation decisions should be driven by governance and compliance, not by unfounded concerns about network inefficiency

This technical insight provides a solid, data-backed foundation for making informed architectural decisions in SAC deployments.

entry.title: [BDC-SAC] IP Whitelist and Access Control in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)
entry.content:
SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) does not provide a native, built-in feature for IP-based whitelist or IP access control at application level. However, IP control can be effectively implemented through Identity Provider (IdP) integration.

IP Control via IdP and SSO Integration

In most customer environments, SAC is integrated with the customer’s existing SSO infrastructure by connecting to an external IdP.
When SAC is accessed through such an SSO setup:

  • IP-based access restrictions can be enforced at the IdP or SSO system level
  • This allows customers to control which IP ranges or network zones are permitted to authenticate and access SAC
  • As a result, while SAC itself does not perform IP filtering, IP access control is commonly achieved through the customer’s identity and authentication layer, which aligns well with enterprise security architectures.

IP Range Characteristics

  • IP ranges used by SAP data centers are fixed (End point)
  • The specific IP range depends on the data center region where the SAC tenant is hosted
  • Customers can identify and validate the relevant IP ranges based on the tenant’s regional deployment

SAP provides an official and up-to-date reference for these IP ranges and endpoints, which can be found here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/regions-and-api-endpoints-available-for-cloud-foundry-environment

entry.title: Scalable Report Broadcasting in SAP Analytics Cloud: Replacing BusinessObjects Scheduling with APOS
entry.content:
I recently delivered a presentation at the SAP Platform and Data Summit in Toronto on our company's data fabric and analytics  modernization journey from SAP BusinessObjects and a HANA sidecar to SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere. My presentation focused on our efforts to replace our BusinessObjects scheduling and broadcasting workflows in SAC.

With the help of our implementation partner SimpleFi Solutions and SAP, the deployment of SAC and Datasphere went very smoothly, but when it came to replicating our BusinessObjects scheduling and broadcasting workflows, we ran into some serious complications.

Landscape

At the start of this process, our landscape looked like this:

  • Our system of record was ECC.
  • We used an SAP Landscape Transformation (SLT) server to replicate data to a HANA sidecar.
  • A BusinessObjects universe was built on top of calculation views.
  • We also used BusinessObjects Data Service for some weekend jobs, inventory jobs, month-end jobs, etc.
  • An SFTP server handled files.
  • We used BI Launchpad for reporting (over 2,000 reports, with no audit system).
  • Our e-commerce team used (and continues to use), the DOMO dashboarding and visualization tool, and data was fed to DOMO from BusinessObjects system. We set up an instance schedule which would feed that data to DOMO.
  • Distributors and sales reps logged in to our B2B website to see inventory and order. The inventory files came in from the BusinessObjects system.

In late 2024, we started our modernization journey, driven by:

  • Cost: How do we save money by reducing the number of systems in play?
  • New functionality: How do we leverage new features in the cloud-based system?
  • BusinessObjects retirement: We wanted to get out of BusinessObjects and deploy SAP Analytics Cloud.

SAP made a very strong case for moving our analytics to the cloud, using Datasphere to replace our HANA sidecar, and SAC to replace BusinessObjects. We moved SAP ECC to ECC on RISE. It was a relatively smooth move.

The modernized data fabric and analytics landscape now looks like this:

Critical Report Challenges

SAP cloud-based data fabric and analytics held great promise, but we hit obstacles with SAP Analytics Cloud Publications. These obstacles are common to cloud-based BI & analytics platforms, but we needed a strategy to replicate our report distribution workflows from BusinessObjects.

SAC Publications limitations:

  • Inability to handle a high volume of reports
  • Inability to handle a high volume of jobs
  • Inability to process large data sets
  • Restrictions on external recipients
  • Inconsistencies in Excel formatting

The new landscape includes APOS Publisher for Cloud, which we implemented to address our broadcasting needs.

We had a number of use cases we needed to replicate in the new landscape, but in summary, these were the requirements:

  • Timely personalized reports to Sales reps (many of whom were external to the company)
  • Large data extracts of up to 200,000 rows
  • Regular updates to DOMO for data integration and analysis
  • Hourly updates via SFTP for operational needs

To meet these use cases, we needed “expanded broadcasting capabilities beyond those available in SAC.

Report Broadcasting with APOS Publisher for Cloud

We assessed our broadcasting needs and reached out to our partner SimpleFi Solutions and to our SAP account executive, who introduced us to APOS. After initial consultations with APOS solution consultants, we entered into a proof of concept. The proof of concept demonstrated that the APOS Publisher for Cloud solution could meet our needs with regard to complexity, volume, formatting, personalization and security.

With the help of APOS, we were able to get to a successful go-live with:

  • All use case requirements met
  • A long-term, scalable report broadcasting solution
  • Simplified scheduling and broadcasting administration
  • Satisfied sales reps and C-level execs

Use Cases

Publisher for Cloud allowed us to fulfill use cases that were not possible with SAC Publications. Here are a few:

  • Use Case #1: Send Open Orders Workbooks to Reps and Account Service Teams
  • Use Case #2: Summarized Data (e.g., Flash Report)
  • Use Case #3: Data Feed to External Reporting System

Use Case #1: Send Open Orders Workbooks to Reps and Account Service Teams

The Open Orders report is a very large report in an Excel workbook. It is extremely important that reps get the report first thing in the morning for their daily activities, and that each rep get only the data to which they are entitled, and in the Excel format to which they are accustomed.

Requirements:

  • Many reps are external 
  • Reps are accustomed and committed to having these reports delivered to their inboxes
  • Formatted Excel

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: Excel spreadsheet with filters and email recipients (Example - Sales Rep1 - Email to john.doe@xyz.com)
  • Format: Pre-defined Excel spreadsheet

Use Case #2: Summarized Data (e.g., Flash Report)

The Summarized Data report goes to C-level executives first thing in the morning, summarizing data from multiple sources (billing information, inventory, MTD data, YTD data, etc.), delivered in a multi-tab Excel workbook.

  • Delivery must be timely and without fail
  • Formatted Excel
  • Executives are accustomed and committed to having these reports delivered to their inboxes

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: Excel spreadsheet with email recipients
  • Format: Multiple pages pointing to different table widgets in the story

Use Case #3: Data Feed to External Reporting System

The data feed to an external reporting system is used to create a near real-time dashboard for management. For this feed, we set up an SAC story and needed to generate and send a report with a pre-determined frequency. Timely generation and execution of this report was critical.

In Publisher for Cloud, we set up a bursting definition with:

  • Source: SAC story
  • Data Source: raw data in CSV or Excel format
  • Format: Pre-defined Excel spreadsheet

Outcomes

The three use cases described above were critical to a successful transformation project, and Publisher for Cloud was instrumental in overcoming the limitations we found in SAC Publications. Publisher for Cloud allowed us to replicate the report distribution workflows that SAP BusinessObjects handled so successfully. With Publisher for Cloud, we achieved:

  • High volume reporting
  • Ability to process large data sets
  • No restrictions on external recipients
  • Consistent Excel formatting

The successful deployment of SAC and Publisher for Cloud means we are free to consider many new use cases to transform our business processes, and Publisher for Cloud’s recent integration with agentic AI opens up endless possibilities for improved analytics workflows

entry.title: New Java Script Event in SAP Analytics Cloud End User File Upload
entry.content:
End User File Upload

In 2024 we shipped the end user file upload in SAP Analytics Cloud planning (see New End User File Upload in SAP Analytics Cloud QRC3 2024). The feature has been well received by our customers and in widely adopted now. Since then we have been working on enhancing the feature (see New Clean and Replace Feature for SAC End User File Upload ) but also still see areas where we can improve, like the necessity to provide a more flexible un-pivoting (see also How to configure a dynamic file upload in SAP Analytics Cloud).

With version SAC QRC1 2026 we provide a new script event, that allows customers to check and massage the uploaded data before it is written into the system and to control error situations flexibly. 

Events in File Upload

So far we had two Java Script events in the file upload: onBeforeExecute and onAfterExecute. The event onBeforeExecute is executed before any of the upload functions is performed whereas onAfterExecute is triggered when everything has finished already. Customer have been asking for an additional event that is triggered during the upload. This event is now delivered with SAC QRC1 2026 and is called onBeforeImport.

 

When is the Event Called? Which information does it receive?

When running a file upload from the file upload starter the user is asked to select the file in a file selector. Once a file has been chosen the content of the file is uploaded to the browser. 

This is the moment the new event is triggered. In your script you have access to the following information:

  • file name of the chosen file (fileName),
  • the content of the file (fileContent),
  • the target version where the data should be written,
  • value of flag for using the first row as headers (firstRowAsHeaders).

The java script can return the object DataUploadFileHandler. In this object you can set two parameters:

  • continue
  • modifiedFileContent.

How can you react?

The first parameter enables you to abort the file upload. By default the value is set to 'true'. If you set the parameter continue to 'false', then the file upload will be stopped.

The second parameter can contain the modified file content. If you leave this parameter blank, then the system will use the originally uploaded data for further processing. In case the parameter is filled, the system will continue with the new content. That means that in your java script you have the option to not only check the uploaded data but even massage the data before further processing. For example, you could:

  • eliminate erroneous records,
  • correct erroneous records,
  • do dynamic un-pivoting, etc.

Please note that you will receive the data as uploaded with no mappings defined in data wrangling applied yet.

Provided that you do not abort the execution by setting the parameter continue to 'false' the system now proceeds with processing the data. The system will check whether a new file content has been provided - in this case it will proceed with the new content. If no new content has been provided, then the system proceeds with the unaltered initially uploaded content.

The data records are then sent to the data wrangling where all the mappings defined in the definition of the job are applied. Finally the data is checked and the correct data is written to the model.

Sample Coding

We want to give a simple example here. Let us assume we are uploading a file with header line (first row of the file consists of the column headers).

This is our sample coding in our script:

 
//1. send an information with the name of the uploaded file
Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Warning, "Uploaded file name is" + fileName );
 
//2. show the content of the uploaded file in the console
for (var i=0;i<fileContent.length;i++){
var lineTest = fileContent[i];
console.log(lineTest);
}
 
//3. massage the uploaded file and only use the first 7 rows (including one header row) for the upload
var UpdatedFile = fileContent.splice(0,7);
var result = cast(Type.DataUploadFileHandler,{continue:true, modifiedFileContent:UpdatedFile});
return result;

In the first block we show an example, how to use the file name of the uploaded file and to display it to the end user /as a warning).

In the second block we show how to access the content of the uploaded file. We loop over the rows and display each row in the console.

In the last block we show how the uploaded data can be modified. We use a modified file content containing only the first 7 rows of the originally uploaded data. As the first row contains the header line we thus only us the first 6 data records from the original file.

Let us run the sample upload with the above script. We trigger the upload starter that is using our script. After our selected file is uploaded to the browser, we get the information which file has been selected.

 Next, we have a look at the console and can view the content of the uploaded data. Our uploaded file containes 10 data records.

And finally, once the upload has finished, we get the information that 6 new records have been written into the selected version.

Thus we can see that indeed the modified data was used that we have stripped down to 6 records (plus the header line).

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud: Best Practices for Folder Organization and Governance
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Analytics Cloud projects, a significant amount of effort is spent on building models, stories, and planning applications. However, one critical topic is often underestimated: how these objects are organized. In many customer environments, reports and models are scattered across different folders without a clear structure, making it difficult to control access, ensure consistency, and maintain content over time. This is not just a usability issue, it is a governance challenge. A well-designed folder structure is a foundational element for scalable, secure, and maintainable SAC landscapes.

 

Why Folder Organization Matters in SAC?

Folder structure is not a cosmetic choice, it is a governance decision.

Number of objects like Stories, Models, Themes, Insights are rapidly increasing in SAC environments. It is crucial to keep these objects in organized way. Otherwise, this can cause important security and governance issues. Without well-planned folder structure,  important objects like corporate stories, models etc. can be located in My Files or Public Folders with ambiguous ownerships. It is not certain that who is using these files and folders and who are the owners. Without discrimination of folders, contents that should be tested first can be accidently used with Production purposes. Also with authorization complexity, unnecessary accesses can cause security issues. As a result, weak folder structure leads low maintainability, low security and high operational risk.

 

Core Folder Types

Understanding the core folder types in SAP Analytics Cloud is essential for building a sustainable governance model. Each folder type serves a specific purpose, and misusing them often leads to security risks, ownership issues, and long-term maintenance problems. The two primary folder types My Files and Public should be clearly differentiated and used intentionally.

My Files – Personal Workspace

My Files is designed as a personal working area for individual users in SAP Analytics Cloud. Content stored in My Files is accessible only by the owner and is not governed by role-based access control, which makes it suitable for early-stage development, drafts, experiments, and personal analysis. It serves as a sandbox environment where users can prototype ideas or perform temporary testing without impacting shared content. However, because objects in My Files are not centrally visible or governed unless explicitly shared, they are not appropriate for long-term or productive use.

As a best practice, any object that is actively used by business users should not located in My Files.

Public – Enterprise Content Area

Public is the central, governed content area in SAP Analytics Cloud and serves as the foundation for enterprise analytics. Unlike personal folders, content stored in Public is managed through role-based access control, enabling controlled visibility, clear ownership, and secure collaboration across teams and departments. Public folders support structured hierarchies, which makes them ideal for implementing environment separation such as DEV, TEST, and PROD, as well as department-based organization. All shared stories, models, planning applications, and related objects intended for long-term use should reside in the Public folder structure. By enforcing consistent naming conventions and access rules within Public, organizations can significantly improve maintainability, reduce operational risk, and establish a scalable governance model for SAP Analytics Cloud.

As a best practice, all productive SAC content should be located in the Public folder hierarchy.

 

DEV – TEST – PROD Folder Strategy

A clear DEV–TEST–PROD folder strategy is one of the most critical elements of governance in SAP Analytics Cloud. Separation  between development - testing and production provides a strong folder discipline. Especially, the separation is crucial if you are in a single tenant landscape. Even in multi-tenant landscapes (Dev-Test-Prod), this is also recommended since the transportation between tenants is not mandatory and system-driven. 

A structured folder strategy introduces a clear content lifecycle, reduces operational risk, and creates transparency for both IT and business users.

Recommended High-Level Folder Structure

/Public
├─ _DEV 
├─ _TEST
└─ _PROD

Environment Responsibilities

DEV

  • Active development and frequent changes
  • Broad access for developers and consultants
  • Experimental and incomplete content allowed

TEST

  • Business validation and user acceptance testing
  • Limited write access
  • Focus on functional correctness and data validation

PROD

  • Approved, production-ready content only
  • Strict access control
  • Stable objects consumed by business users

Object Type-Based Folder Design

Standardized subfolder design based on object types is a key enabler for consistency and scalability in SAP Analytics Cloud. By structuring folders according to object types—such as Stories, Models, Analytic Applications, Data Actions, and Allocations—organizations create a predictable and intuitive navigation experience across DEV, TEST, and PROD environments.

Department-Based Folder Design

By grouping stories, models, and applications under department-specific folders such as Finance, Sales, Supply Chain, or HR, ownership and accountability become immediately clear. This approach simplifies access management through role-based authorization, reduces the risk of unintended data exposure, and improves discoverability for business users. When combined with a DEV–TEST–PROD structure, department-based folders ensure that each team works within a clearly defined and governed space, supporting scalability and long-term maintainability of the SAC environment.

 

Workspaces

Workspaces in SAP Analytics Cloud are designed to support collaboration by grouping content, users, and access rights within a shared space. They can be useful for project-based or temporary initiatives where multiple users work closely on the same set of stories or models.

However, from a governance perspective, I personally avoid using Workspaces as a primary content container. When a Workspace is deleted, all content stored within it is permanently removed, which introduces a significant operational risk. For this reason, Workspaces should be used selectively and primarily for short-lived collaboration, while long-term and productive content should always reside in the Public folder structure.

You can navigate to this blog post by @ninak1 to learn workspaces.

 

Authorization Best Practices (Team-Based Governance)

Effective authorization in SAP Analytics Cloud should be managed through teams, not individual users. Assigning access directly to users quickly becomes unmanageable as environments grow, leading to inconsistent permissions, security risks, and high maintenance effort. A team-based authorization model provides scalability, transparency, and long-term governance by clearly separating responsibilities and standardizing access patterns.

Recommended Team Personas

A clear persona model is essential for aligning technical permissions with business responsibilities. The following team structure covers most SAC scenarios while remaining simple and scalable:

Admin

  • System and tenant administration
  • User, team, and role management
  • Full access across DEV, TEST, and PROD

Developer

  • Model, story, and application development
  • Full write access in DEV
  • Limited or no write access in PROD

Editor / Analyst 

  • Ability to create own stories and perform small adjustments
  • Typically business power users or advanced analysts
  • Write access to specific folders, without system-level permissions

Why “Editor / Analyst”?
Compared to “Developer,” this role better reflects business-driven content creation and controlled flexibility without exposing technical or administrative capabilities.

Viewer

  • Read-only access to validated, productive content
  • No content creation or modification rights

Folder Authorization via Teams

Folders should always be shared with teams, not individual users. This approach ensures that access rights are role-based, auditable, and easy to maintain.

Best Practices

  • DEV folders → Developer team (edit), Admin team (full)
  • TEST folders → Developer / Editor (limited edit), Viewer (read)
  • PROD folders → Viewer (read), Editor (controlled edit), Admin (full)

By assigning folder permissions at the team level, access changes are handled simply by adding or removing users from teams—without touching folder settings.

Role Assignment Strategy

In addition to folder access, SAC roles define what users can do at the platform level. These roles should also be assigned to teams rather than individuals.

Example Role Mapping

  • Admin team → BI Admin role
  • Developer team → Content Creator role
  • Editor / Analyst team → Content Creator (restricted by folder access)
  • Viewer team → Viewer role

Best Practice:
Assign roles and folder permissions to teams—not to individual users.

Here is the link of the detailed blog post for Security Concept by @Matthew_Shaw

 

What to Do Next

To apply these best practices in your SAP Analytics Cloud environment, start with the following steps:

  • Define your governance model first
    Agree on how content should be developed, validated, and consumed before creating folders.

  • Separate DEV, TEST, and PROD clearly
    Create dedicated environment folders under Public and enforce a forward-only content flow.

  • Organize content by department and object type
    Use department-based folders combined with standardized subfolders (Stories, Models, Data Actions, etc.).

  • Establish a clear naming convention
    Document and communicate a simple, consistent naming standard for all SAC objects.

  • Adopt team-based authorization
    Create teams based on personas (Admin, Developer, Editor/Analyst, Viewer) and assign both roles and folder permissions to teams—not individual users.

  • Review and clean up existing content
    Identify unused, duplicated, or misplaced objects and move productive content into the governed Public structure.

 

Sources

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-analytics-cloud-content-organization-and-authorization/ba-p/13350602
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-analytics-cloud-security-concepts-and-best-practice/ba-p/13416999
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-analytics-cloud-workspaces/ba-p/13531009

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 差異の表示
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、ストーリーにて予実差異を表示したい！前年度差異をぱっとわかるようにしたい！といった方向けに、以下のようにチャートやテーブルにおいて差異を表示する方法についてご紹介します。

 目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示
  • チャートでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］
  • テーブルでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成手順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。

※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、sa操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年2月に作成しています。）

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける差異表示は大きく2種類：チャートでの差異表示とテーブルでの差異計算列の追加による表示があります。

チャートでの差異表示では、複数のバージョン間での差異、または複数の期間における差異を値またはパーセントで表示することができます。差異の表現方法は、チャートによってデータラベルや累積、棒グラフやウォーターフォールなど、見たい形によって使い分けることができます。
　
詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：チャートに対する差異の追加 | SAP Help Portal

テーブルでは、計算列または計算列を追加することで差異含め様々な計算された値を表示することができます。
クライアント計算で追加できる一般的な式は以下になります。

詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：テーブルセルでのクライアント計算の作成 (新しいテーブル構築エクスペリエンス) | SAP Help Portal

チャートでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成で作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。
0.2. 「Page_1」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「複製」をクリックしてページを複製します。

0.3. 「Page_1 (1)」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「タイトルの編集」をクリックし、タイトルを「差異」に変更します。

 

 

1. 数値ポイントのチャートに、前年度との差異を追加しよう！

 

 

1.1. 数値ポイントチャートをクリックした上、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

 

1.2. （2025年の第2四半期にフィルタをかけます。）「＋フィルタの追加」をクリックします。

 

1.3. 「Data（メンバー）」をクリックします。

 

1.4. 「2025」を展開して「Q2」を選択し、「OK」をクリックします。（2025年の第2四半期にフィルタがかかりました。）

 

1.5. ビルダパネルの「チャートアドオン」を展開し、「差異」をクリックします。

 

1.6. 「＋バージョン/時間の追加」をクリックします。

 1.7. 「Date」を選択します。（今回は前年度差異のためDateを選択しましたが、予実差異の場合はバージョンを選択します。）

 

 

1.8. TO (B)の時間にて、「前の時間間隔」の「ｖ」をクリックします。

1.9. 「前年度」を選択します。（これにより、2025Q2との比較から2024Q2との比較に変更されました。）

 

1.10. 表示オプションの差異の表示形式を「パーセント」、小数部を「1」に変更し、右下の「処理済み」をクリックします。


1.11. 前年度差異を％で表すチャートが完成しました！

2. 棒チャートに、予算と実績の差異を追加しよう！

 

 2.1.数値ポイントチャートをクリックした上、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

2.2. 色の「＋ディメンション/メジャーの追加」をクリックします。

 

2.3. 「バージョン」を選択します。

2.4. 「＋バージョンの追加」をクリックし、「Budget」を選択します。（チャートに実績と予算の両方が表示されました。）


2.5.  ビルダパネルの「チャートアドオン」を展開し、「差異」をクリックします。


2.6. 「＋バージョン/時間の追加」をクリックします。

2.7. 「バージョン」を選択します。

2.8. 表示オプションの差異の表示形式を「パーセント」に変更し、右下の「処理済み」をクリックします。


2.9. 予実差異を％で確認できるチャートが完成しました！

 

テーブルでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］

3. テーブルにパーセント差異の列を追加しよう！

3.1. テーブルをクリックした上で右パネル（ビルたパネル）を開き、列の「＋ディメンションの追加」をクリックします。

3.2. 「バージョン」を選択します。 



3.3. フィルタの「Version」をクリックし、「Budget」にチェックを入れて「OK」をクリックします。

3.4.  テーブル内の「Actual」と「Budget」の2つのセルを同時選択して右クリックし、「クライアント計算の追加」>「パーセント差異」>「繰返」をクリックします。

3.5. 追加された「パーセント差異」のセルをダブルクリックし、名前を「予算比」に変更します。

3.6. 予算比が表示されるようになりました！

 

3.7. 左上の「保存」をクリックして、ストーリーを保存します。

 

今回は差異の表示方法の紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。
はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Displaying SAP Analytics Cloud KPI Tiles from Stories Using REST APIs
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is widely used by organizations to provide interactive storytelling and track the business KPIs with advanced visualizations. However, companies often need to obtain KPI information in ways other than the SAC user interface, including in custom web apps. Although SAC does not support the direct embedding of KPI tiles into external apps, it does provide REST APIs that allow programmatic access to widget-level data from SAC stories. These APIs can be used to collect and display KPI tile data, including number (value), number state (status), title, and subtitle, in a bespoke user interface.

In this blog, I walk through a detailed, end-to-end implementation that illustrates how to fetch KPI tile data from a SAP Analytics Cloud story using the widgetquery/getWidgetData REST API. The approach uses Python for backend processing and Flask as a lightweight web framework to securely call SAC APIs and output KPI values on a web page.

Configuration of the Project

We may test API access and retrieve KPI tile data using a straightforward Python script before developing the Flask application. This program shows you how to:

  1. Use OAuth 2.0 to authenticate with SAC
  2. Acquire a token of access
  3. Use the SAC widgetquery/getWidgetData RESTAPI.
  4. Show the console’s KPI values
import requests
import webbrowser
import urllib.parse

# ---------------- CONFIG ----------------
TENANT_URL = "https://<your-tenant>.hanacloudservices.cloud.sap"

CLIENT_ID = "<YOUR_CLIENT_ID>"
CLIENT_SECRET = "<YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET>"

AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = "https://<your-tenant>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/authorize"
TOKEN_ENDPOINT = "https://<your-tenant>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"

REDIRECT_URI = "https://your-app-domain.com/oauth/callback"  # used only to capture code manually

STORY_ID = "<your-storyid>"
WIDGET_IDS = [
    "Chart_1", "Chart_2", "Chart_3",
    "Chart_4", "Chart_5", "Chart_6", "Chart_8"
]

# ---------------- STEP 1: LOGIN ----------------
params = {
    "response_type": "code",
    "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
    "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI
}

auth_url = AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)

print("\n Opening browser for SAC login...")
webbrowser.open(auth_url)

code = input("\n Paste the authorization code here: ").strip()

# ---------------- STEP 2: TOKEN ----------------
payload = {
    "grant_type": "authorization_code",
    "code": code,
    "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI,
    "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
    "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET
}

token_resp = requests.post(
    TOKEN_ENDPOINT,
    data=payload,
    headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"}
)

token_resp.raise_for_status()
access_token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

print("\n Access token received")

# ---------------- STEP 3: FETCH KPI ----------------
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Accept": "application/json"
}

print("\n KPI VALUES\n" + "-" * 40)

for widget_id in WIDGET_IDS:
    url = f"{TENANT_URL}/widgetquery/getWidgetData"
    params = {
        "storyId": STORY_ID,
        "widgetId": widget_id,
        "type": "kpiTile"
    }

    r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
    if r.ok:
        data = r.json()
        number = data.get("number", "N/A")
        title = data.get("title", widget_id)
        print(f"{title}: {number}")
    else:
        print(f"{widget_id}:  Error")
print("\n Done")

 How this operate

  1. Login Step: Launches a web browser to obtain the permission code and log into SAC.
  2. Token Step: Provides an access token in exchange for the authorization code.
  3. Fetch KPI Step: Prints the KPI number and title after contacting the SAC REST API for each widget ID.

 

Code of Application

The full Flask application that is used to retrieve KPI tile data and authenticate with SAP Analytics Cloud is shown below. This code manages widget data fetching, token retrieval, login, and creates an eye-catching KPI dashboard in the browser.

HTML_TEMPLATE = """
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
<title>SAC KPI Dashboard</title>
<style>
    body {
        font-family: 'Segoe UI', Tahoma, Geneva, Verdana, sans-serif;
        background: #f4f6f8;
        margin: 0; padding: 0;
    }
    h1 { text-align: center; margin-top: 20px; color: #333; }
    .container {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        justify-content: center;
        margin: 40px auto;
        max-width: 1200px;
        gap: 20px;
    }
    .card {
        color: #fff;
        width: 250px;
        height: 150px;
        border-radius: 16px;
        box-shadow: 0 10px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.2);
        display: flex;
        flex-direction: column;
        justify-content: center;
        align-items: center;
        transition: transform 0.3s, box-shadow 0.3s;
        cursor: pointer;
        text-align: center;
        padding: 10px;
    }
    .card:hover {
        transform: translateY(-10px);
        box-shadow: 0 20px 30px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
    }
    .number {
        font-weight: bold;
        white-space: nowrap;
        overflow: hidden;
        text-overflow: ellipsis;
        max-width: 90%;
    }
    .title {
        font-size: 1em;
        margin-top: 10px;
        color: #e0e0e0;
    }
    /* Rainbow colors for cards */
    .rainbow-0 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ff6b6b, #f06595); }
    .rainbow-1 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #feca57, #ff9f43); }
    .rainbow-2 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1dd1a1, #10ac84); }
    .rainbow-3 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #54a0ff, #2e86de); }
    .rainbow-4 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #5f27cd, #341f97); }
    .rainbow-5 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ee5253, #c0392b); }
    .rainbow-6 { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #48dbfb, #00d2d3); }
</style>
<script>
    // Adjust font size based on length
    function adjustFontSize() {
        const numbers = document.querySelectorAll('.number');
        numbers.forEach(num => {
            const length = num.innerText.length;
            if(length <= 5) num.style.fontSize = '2.5em';
            else if(length <= 8) num.style.fontSize = '2em';
            else num.style.fontSize = '1.5em';
        });
    }
    window.onload = adjustFontSize;
</script>
</head>
<body>
<h1>SAP Analytics Cloud - RESTAPI Fetched Sales KPI Dashboard</h1>
<div class="container">
    {% for kpi in kpis %}
    <div class="card rainbow-{{ loop.index0 % 7 }}">
        <div class="number">{{ kpi.number }}</div>
        <div class="title">{{ kpi.title }}</div>
    </div>
    {% endfor %}
</div>
</body>
</html>
"""
from flask import Flask, render_template_string
import requests
import urllib.parse
import webbrowser

# ---------------- CONFIG ----------------
TENANT_URL = "https://yourtenant.hanacloudservices.cloud.sap"
CLIENT_ID = "<YOUR_CLIENT_ID>"
CLIENT_SECRET = "<YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET>"
AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = "https://yourtenant.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/authorize"
TOKEN_ENDPOINT = "https://yourtenant.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
REDIRECT_URI = "https://your-app-domain.com/oauth/callback"

STORY_ID = "<your_storyid>"
WIDGET_IDS = [
    "Chart_1", "Chart_2", "Chart_3",
    "Chart_4", "Chart_5", "Chart_6", "Chart_8"
]

# ---------------- FLASK APP ----------------
app = Flask(__name__)

def get_access_token():
    # Step 1: login manually
    params = {"response_type": "code", "client_id": CLIENT_ID, "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI}
    auth_url = AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)
    print("\nOpen this URL in browser to login to SAC:")
    print(auth_url)
    webbrowser.open(auth_url)
    code = input("\nPaste the authorization code here: ").strip()

    # Step 2: get token
    payload = {
        "grant_type": "authorization_code",
        "code": code,
        "redirect_uri": REDIRECT_URI,
        "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET
    }
    r = requests.post(TOKEN_ENDPOINT, data=payload, headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"})
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["access_token"]

def fetch_kpis(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Accept": "application/json"}
    kpis = []
    for widget_id in WIDGET_IDS:
        url = f"{TENANT_URL}/widgetquery/getWidgetData"
        params = {"storyId": STORY_ID, "widgetId": widget_id, "type": "kpiTile"}
        r = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
        if r.ok:
            data = r.json()
            kpis.append({
                "title": data.get("title", widget_id),
                "number": data.get("number", "N/A")
            })
        else:
            kpis.append({"title": widget_id, "number": "Error"})
    return kpis
# HTML Code will be written Here
@app.route("/")
def dashboard():
    token = get_access_token()
    kpis = fetch_kpis(token)
    return render_template_string(HTML_TEMPLATE, kpis=kpis)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True,port=8000)

 An explanation of the code

  1. /route logic
    • To manually retrieve an OAuth token, use get_access_token().
    • Uses fetch_kpis() to retrieve KPI tile data.
    • Uses the HTML_TEMPLATE to render all KPIs with rainbow-colored cards.
  2. Managing OAuth (get_access_token)
    • Launches the browser's SAC login.
    • The authorization code is pasted by the user. To call SAC REST APIs, code is exchanged for access tokens.
  3. Data retrieval for widgets (fetch_kpis)
    • Repeats over every WIDGET_IDS.
    • For every widget, the widgetquery/getWidgetData endpoint is called.
    • Gathers the number and title for the display.
  4. Using HTML Templates for UI rendering
    • Flex arrangement is used for tiles.
    • Each KPI card has a rainbow gradient background.
    • For a contemporary appearance, use shadow and hover effects.
    • The length of the integer automatically modifies the font size.

Result/Output

The KPI data is retrieved and shown in a personalized web dashboard after the program has launched and the user has successfully logged in using SAP Analytics Cloud OAuth.

SAP Analytics Cloud Story KPI Tiles

The original KPI tiles as they appear in the SAP Analytics Cloud narrative are depicted in the image below. Business users in SAC are in charge of configuring and maintaining these KPIs.

 

Custom Web Dashboard with KPI Tiles

The widgetquery/getWidgetData REST API is used to retrieve the same KPI values, which are then shown in a specially created web application. Custom Web Dashboard Screenshot

Important Findings

The KPI numbers in the SAC story and the web dashboard are same.
Every KPI tile shows:

  1. Value or Number 
  2. The title

The website's dashboard uses the following to improve visualization:

  1. Gradient cards with rainbows
  2. Hover animations and shadows
  3. Adaptable design

This proves to the secure consumption and reuse of SAC KPI data outside of the SAC user interface without requiring the duplication of business logic.

Conclusion

In this blog post, we shown a simple yet effective technique for extracting SAP REST APIs are used to tile Analytics Cloud KPI data and display it on a special webpage.

Important findings:

  • SAC KPIs can be consumed using custom dashboards outside of the SAC UI.

  • Python provides an adaptable and lightweight backend for API integration. REST APIs enable KPI tracking in real-time, while front-end. Style increases visibility.

  • Setting up the environment and security is essential for a safe execution.

  • SAC insights can be incorporated into executive dashboards, intranet portals, or external web apps, businesses are able to give users a consolidated view of key performance indicators without having to Launch SAC directly.

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - しきい値の設定
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、しきい値を設定し、その値を超えているのか、例えばある目標値を達成しているのかや予算比がプラスなのかマイナスなのか、といったことをぱっとみて把握できるようにする方法についてご紹介します。

 
 目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおけるしきい値の設定
  • テーブルでのしきい値による色分け方法［ハンズオン］

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成手順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。

※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年2月に作成しています。）

SAP Analytics Cloudにおけるしきい値の設定

SAP Analytics Cloudでは、しきい値を設けて範囲ごとに色分けして表現することができます。範囲は複数設定することができるため、細かくしきい値を分けて管理することができます。また色の表現方法は、数字の横にマークとして表現するだけでなく、数値字体や背景の色を反映させることができます。

詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：テーブルでのしきい値の使用 | SAP Help Portal

テーブルでのしきい値による色分け方法［ハンズオン］

このハンズオンは、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 差異の表示 の「テーブルでの差異の追加方法［ハンズオン］」の続きになります。上記のハンズオンを完了してから以下のハンズオンを実施してください。

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 差異の表示で編集したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。

1. テーブル上の予算比にしきい値を設定して色分けしよう！

しきい値は、テーブルに直接追加するか、ビルダパネルを使用して追加することができますが、今回はテーブルに直接追加する方法をご紹介します。

1.1. テーブルの「予算比」のセルを右クリックし、「しきい値を追加」>「＋新規しきい値」をクリックします。

1.2. 今回は予算比がプラスの場合は緑色、マイナスの場合は赤色となるようにしきい値を設定します。
新規しきい値の範囲にて、最小に「0」と入力し、「範囲の追加」をクリックします。

1.3.  新しく追加した範囲の色を赤色に変更します。

1.4. 赤色の範囲の最大に「0」と入力し、Dateのフィルタを「×」で解除して「適用」をクリックします。

1.5. 予算比がプラスかマイナスかがぱっとみてわかるようになりました！

1.6. (オプション) 右パネル（スタイルパネル）を開き、「しきい値のスタイル」をお好きなものに変更します。

 

 

今回はしきい値のご紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: What's New in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 Analytics - January 2026
entry.content:
As part of our monthly releases during Q4 2025, we have introduced a new set of analytics innovations across SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management. These enhancements reflect our continued investment in scalable, global analytics and address some of the most frequently requested capabilities from our customers.

The Q4 focus was clear: extend analytics beyond a single product, enable global usability, and empower organizations with more flexible, enterprise-grade insights. This release delivers on that vision with three key innovations:

  • Metadata Translation Support for Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Stories
  • Currency Conversion 1.0
  • Live Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition (Restricted GA)

Below is a closer look at how each of these enhancements delivers tangible business value.

Metadata Translation Support for Embedded SAC Stories

SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management are deployed globally, supporting users across regions, languages, and cultures. To ensure consistent and localized user experiences, both solutions offer Language Adaptation, enabling administrators to translate field labels, code lists, UI texts, and custom messages.

Until now, Embedded SAC supported data translations and translations created directly within SAC’s Query Designer and Story Designer. With the November 2025 release, metadata translations maintained centrally in the Language Adaptation tool are now automatically applied to Embedded SAC Stories.

Business impact:

  • Single point of translation maintenance across operational UIs and analytics
  • Consistent multilingual experiences for business users
  • Reduced administrative overhead by eliminating duplicate translation efforts

All translated field-related metadata is seamlessly reflected in the Embedded SAC user interface, ensuring analytics remain as intuitive and localized as the underlying applications.

You can see how this works in this video.

Currency Conversion 1.0

For organizations operating across multiple regions, currency conversion is a foundational analytics requirement—particularly in sales and service reporting. With the December 2025 release, we introduce Currency Conversion 1.0 for Embedded SAC.

Administrators can now define a target currency for amount-based attributes:

  • During data modeling in the Query Designer
  • Directly in the Story Designer for specific charts or tables

Different target currencies can be applied within the same story, offering greater flexibility for global and regional reporting.

Key capabilities and considerations:

  • Centralized rate maintenance: Conversion rates maintained in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 or SAP Enterprise Service Management are automatically used in SAC stories.
  • Automatic rate selection: Conversion rates are applied at runtime; users do not manage conversion datasets manually.
  • Modeling requirement: Both amount and currency attributes must be included in the data source for conversion to work correctly.

This capability enables consistent, accurate financial reporting while preserving a single source of truth for currency data. Stay tuned for the ability to select a currency conversion date as the next enhancement to this feature!

You can see how this works in this video.

Live Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition (Restricted GA)

Modern enterprises rely on multiple SAP solutions—such as SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 for CRM and SAP SuccessFactors for HCM—and require a unified analytics layer across systems. SAP Analytics Cloud, Enterprise Edition fulfills this role.

With the December 2025 release, SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management can now integrate with Enterprise SAC via live connections.

Key benefits of live integration:

  • Real-time insights: Data is accessed directly from the source systems during story execution.
  • Enhanced data security: Data remains within the source system and is discarded once the story is closed.
  • Complete data access: All available data sources in Sales & Service Cloud V2 and Enterprise Service Management are accessible in Enterprise SAC.
  • Advanced analytics and modeling: Leverage Enterprise SAC capabilities to further model and visualize operational data.

This feature is currently available under restricted general availability. Customers interested in early access can request enablement by creating a case under component CEC-CRM-ANA. Broader availability is planned in the near future.

You can see how this works in this video.

Help documentation from SAP Analytics Cloud is available for this as well & can be accessed here & here.

Enabling Truly Global, Enterprise-Grade Analytics

Together, these enhancements significantly strengthen the analytics foundation of SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 and SAP Enterprise Service Management. By improving multilingual support, enabling multi-currency reporting, and unlocking enterprise-wide analytics through live integration, we are helping customers scale insights globally—without compromising consistency, security, or performance.

These innovations represent another step toward delivering analytics that are not only powerful, but truly global.

entry.title: Gamify Your Classroom with Business Builders —powered by SAP Analytics Cloud; designed for educators
entry.content:
In a world powered by AI and data, students need more than technical skills—they need to think critically, communicate clearly, and make decisions based on data. Business Builders helps educators bring these skills to life through engaging, scenario-based games—powered by SAP Analytics Cloud and designed by educators for educators.

What is Business Builders?

Business Builders is a free, cloud-based platform that turns data literacy and business storytelling into an interactive learning experience. Students explore real-world scenarios, use SAP Analytics Cloud to analyze data, and compete on leaderboards—earning instant feedback and celebrating success.

Each game challenges students to:

  • Visualize and interpret real business data
  • Answer scenario-specific questions
  • Climb the leaderboard through accurate, timely analysis

Scenarios range from foundational business skills to advanced decision-making—ideal for classrooms focused on analytics, strategy, finance, IT, sustainability, and more.

Who is it for?

Business Builders is built for higher education instructors across all academic levels and disciplines. Whether you teach intro-level undergrads or MBA students, the platform adapts to your classroom.

  • No cost for educators or students
  • No software installation required
  • Built-in gamification to boost engagement
  • Easy setup of students via instructor invitation links

What's in it for your students?

Students will:

  • Learn data analysis and visualization with SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Develop analytical thinking and problem-solving skills as well as data storytelling
  • Collaborate and compete in a game-based environment
  • Build job-ready capabilities for internships and careers

Available Game Scenarios

  • Supply Chain Management
  • International Expansion
  • Sustainability Portfolio
  • The Maple Heir (Business Processes Micro-Challenges)
  • The Agent
  • The Process Detective

Are you a professor/ lecturer or you would love to play Business Builders with students? Check out our BusinessBuilders and register now for a free instructor account! OR in case you have some questions, check out our materials, and one-pager in our Digital Library - Business Builders 

Any questions? Write to universityalliances@sap.com 

 

We look so much forward to hearing from you and your experience with Business Builders! 

Find here below a sample question for your reference

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Certification: A New Era of Skill Validation
entry.content:
SAP has completely redesigned the SAP Analytics Cloud certification, and with it, the way professional skills are assessed. 
The new SAP Analytics Cloud certification is no longer based on multiple-choice questions. There is no proctor, and it is open-book. Candidates engage in an AI-driven role-play exam designed to mirror how professionals actually work with SAP Analytics Cloud. You can prepare with our free learning content available on learning.sap.com.
 
How the new exam works
During the exam, you interact with an AI that asks 20 questions related to SAP Analytics Cloud. 
Key features of the new exam format include:
  • Immediate results: Once you complete your tasks, your performance is reviewed by AI, and you receive your result instantly.
  • Open book format: You can use documentation and resources just like you would in real work situations.
